periods.note: Carthaginian and Iberian/Celtic indigenous; Phoenician and Greek coastal colonies

ethnic_composition.presence: ruling; Iberia one of Rome's most thoroughly Latinized provinces

periods.note: Visigothic Kingdom — Germanic Visigoths; converted to Catholic Christianity 587 AD
Al-Andalus (Umayyad → taifa kingdoms → Almoravid/Almohad → Nasrid Granada) + Christian reconquista kingdoms (Castile, Aragon, Portugal) — coexisting and competing

ethnic_composition.presence: significant military (most of the Muslim army was Berber)
majority throughout; Mozarabs (Christian under Muslim rule) in south; Christian kingdoms in north

periods.note: Habsburg Spain — the marriage of Ferdinand and Isabella united Castile and Aragon; Columbus's voyages (1492) opened the Americas; expulsion of Jews (1492) and Muslims (1502–1614) created the 'pure blood' (limpieza de sangre) obsession. Charles I (= Charles V, HRE) made Spain the core of a global empire. Philip II: Spanish Armada defeated (1588); Dutch revolt; Inquisition. Spain dominated Europe and the Americas but bled itself through endless wars. The Habsburg line ended with Charles II (the Bewitched).

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — Spanish and Portuguese Catholic identity consolidated
minority with distinct non-Indo-European language; provided many early conquistadors

periods.note: Bourbon Spain — War of the Spanish Succession (1701–1714) replaced the Habsburgs with the French Bourbon dynasty; Spain lost its Italian and Low Countries possessions. Bourbon reforms modernised administration. Portugal (united with Spain 1580–1640) re-established independence; Pombal's Portugal rebuilt Lisbon after the 1755 earthquake and expelled the Jesuits.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — Spanish and Portuguese national identities

periods.note: Napoleonic occupation and independence wars — Napoleon installed his brother Joseph as King of Spain (1808); the Peninsular War (1808–1814) was a brutal guerrilla conflict; Wellington's British forces expelled the French. Spanish American colonies used the chaos to achieve independence (1810–1825) — Spain lost its entire American empire in 15 years.

Constitutional Spain — 19th century marked by chronic instability: Carlist Wars (1833–1840, 1846–1849, 1872–1876), military pronunciamentos, First Republic (1873–74). Loss of Cuba, Puerto Rico, and Philippines to USA (1898) — the 'Disaster'. Second Republic (1931–1936); Spanish Civil War (1936–1939) — Franco's Nationalists (backed by Hitler and Mussolini) defeated the Republic (backed by USSR and International Brigades).

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — Castilian Spanish majority; Catalan, Basque, Galician minorities with distinct languages

periods.note: Franco's Spain and modern democracy — Francisco Franco's dictatorship (1939–1975) was the longest-surviving Fascist-aligned regime in Europe. Repression of Catalan and Basque language and culture. Spain remained neutral in WWII (exhausted). Economic development from 1960s; tourist boom. Franco died 1975; Spain transitioned peacefully to democracy (La Transición); EU member 1986. ETA Basque separatist terrorism (1959–2018).

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — Castilian Spanish; Catalan, Basque, and Galician regional autonomy restored
